Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Medieval Latin Scholasticism

Peter Auriol

c. 1280 – 1322

Peter Auriol’s thought on mind is not a minor annex to his theology.

Peter Auriol’s thought on mind is not a minor annex to his theology. It is one of the most distinctive late-medieval attempts to explain cognition without making the mind primarily a container of inner representations. A comprehensive philosophy of mind can be extracted from him if we treat his theological cases — God, angels, the blessed, and beatific vision — as philosophically decisive boundary cases. They show what cognition is when it is not constrained by bodily phantasms, temporal discursiveness, or created representational media.

The core thesis is this: to cognize is for something to appear to a cognitive power. Auriol’s philosophy of mind is therefore an appearance theory of cognition, but not a simple phenomenalism. The object that appears is not, in the ordinary case, a private mental image standing between knower and world. It is the thing itself under a special intentional or apparent mode of being: esse apparens, esse intentionale, esse obiectivum, sometimes also described as being placed “before the gaze of the mind.” This allows Auriol to combine a strong doctrine of cognitive appearance with an anti-representational or direct-realist aspiration.

A textual caution is important. Auriol’s corpus is unevenly edited: the 1596 Roman edition of the Scriptum super primum Sententiarum is described by the Peter Auriol Homepage as unreliable in many places; a large Electronic Scriptum project exists to improve access, while other works, including Reportationes, Quodlibetal material, and the De principiis naturae, remain complex from the standpoint of textual transmission. The most important loci for philosophy of mind include Scriptum I, distinctions 9, 23, 27, and 35, as well as texts on angelic intellect, beatific vision, intellectual memory, and singular cognition.

1. The fundamental account: cognition as apparition

Auriol repeatedly defines cognition through apparere: cognition is that by which an object appears to someone. Recent work by Giovanni Fornasieri and Guillaume Taieb emphasizes that this is not a loose metaphor but the center of Auriol’s account. Taieb summarizes Auriol’s definition as “something which makes an object appear to someone,” while Fornasieri interprets intellectual cognition as the appearance of the thing to a conscious cognizer through connotation: the object is not merely represented but appears as cognized.

This gives Auriol a general theory of mind across sensory, intellectual, angelic, blessed, and divine cases. The unifying question is always: under what mode does the object appear, to what sort of cognitive power, and with what dependence on species, phantasms, attention, or divine essence? André Martin’s recent study on intellectual memory stresses that, for Auriol, “having something present through the mode of appearance” applies not only to human intellectual acts but to cognition broadly, including sensitive, intellective, creaturely, and divine cognition.

This is why Auriol cannot be reduced to a theory of inner mental representations. The cognitive act does not primarily terminate in a private item inside the soul. Rather, it makes the object itself appear. The thing has real being outside the soul and apparent or intentional being as appearing. On this view, the rose I see or think is not replaced by a mental rose; rather, the rose itself is present under an intentional aspect. This is the key to both Auriol’s originality and the difficulties that later critics found in his account.

2. Apparent being and the object of thought

Auriol is a conceptualist about universals but not a subjectivist in a simple sense. He holds that only singulars have real extramental existence, while universals are products or phenomena of cognition. Yet universal concepts are not free-floating mental tokens. They are things themselves — or collections of singular things under a common ratio — as appearing to the intellect. The Stanford Encyclopedia’s account of Auriol emphasizes that, for him, all cognition puts its object into esse apparens and that concepts are not inner representations, intelligible species, mental tokens, or acts taken as the object known; rather, the concept is the extramental particular or particulars under apparent being.

This is the heart of Auriol’s intentional ontology. The same thing can be considered under real being and under apparent being. As real, Socrates exists in the world. As cognized, Socrates is not duplicated as another real entity inside the mind; Socrates appears. Russell Friedman describes this as Auriol’s doctrine that the cognitive faculty “converts” the object into apparent being: Socrates can have both real existence and apparent existence. Friedman also notes the philosophical pressure this creates: if apparent being is too robust, it risks doubling reality or creating a perceptual veil; if it is too thin, it may not explain cognition at all.

Auriol’s solution relies heavily on connotation. A thing-as-cognized does not differ from the thing absolutely, as if there were two substances or two real objects. It differs by a relational or connotative aspect: the thing is signified together with its being cognized. Taieb’s reconstruction makes this especially clear: for created cognizers, cognition is ontologically an absolute complex involving cognitive power and likeness, while the relation to the object is conceptual or representational rather than a new real relation. For God, by contrast, cognition is not a created complex at all but the deity itself.

This gives Auriol a subtle position. He is not a naïve realist who thinks cognition is mere contact with the world without intentional structure. He is also not a representationalist who says we know internal images first and external things only indirectly. He tries to occupy a middle position: the object known is the thing itself, but the thing is known only insofar as it has the intentional mode of appearing.

3. The “two subjects” of intellection: Auriol and Averroes

A valuable non-Anglophone contribution comes from Laura Moaté’s French study of Auriol’s “theory of the two subjects of intellection.” Moaté argues that Auriol develops an original theory of intention by reworking Averroes’s account of the two subjects of intellection. For Averroes, the intelligible has one subject by which it is true — the form in images or phantasms — and another by which it is a form or real entity — the material intellect. Auriol adapts this structure but shifts the emphasis: it is not intellection itself that has two subjects, but intentional being.

In Auriol’s version, the intentional being of the object has a double anchoring. On one side, it depends on the extramental thing according to its real being. On the other, it depends on the thing as present in phantasm or in the intellect’s cognitive field. Moaté cites Auriol’s claim that intentional being has a double subject and a double being: the thing in real existence and the thing in apparent, objective, or phantasmed being. This is a crucial interpretive point because it helps prevent two oversimplifications. Auriol is not saying that cognition floats free from real objects; nor is he saying that cognition is just a passive reception of the real object without cognitive constitution.

Moaté’s interpretation also helps explain why Auriol is so difficult to classify. He is direct-realist in intention: the phantasm is not what is intellected but that by which the thing is intellected. Yet he also insists that cognition has its own intentional structure. This produces a persistent tension: Auriol wants the object to remain continuous with the real thing, but he also gives it an intentional mode that can seem to split the object into real being and apparent being.

4. Faculty psychology: powers distinguished by modes of appearing

Auriol inherits the broad medieval faculty framework: external senses, common sense, imagination, memory, estimative or cogitative power, agent intellect, possible intellect, appetite, will. Yet he revises the standard way of distinguishing faculties. The decisive difference among cognitive powers is not simply that each has a wholly different object. The same thing can be sensed, imagined, remembered, and intellected. What differs is the mode of appearing.

This is one of Auriol’s most important contributions to philosophy of mind. A later Spanish-language and scholastic reception of Auriol, discussed in work on Antonio Pérez, notes that Auriol does not accept the simple Aristotelian claim that powers of the soul are distinguished by their objects. Rather, powers differ according to the way or mode in which the object appears: what can be grasped by the senses can also be grasped by imagination and intellect, though under different modes.

Lukáš Lička’s Czech study of Auriol emphasizes the same point from another angle. Auriol ties life to cognition: living beings are those to whom things appear. Cognitive powers differ not merely in external objects but in first-person modes of manifestation. Seeing, imagining, remembering, and understanding are not distinguished because they always have separate objects, but because the object is present differently in each case.

This has deep systematic consequences. Auriol’s philosophy of mind is not object-taxonomy first; it is phenomenological in a medieval sense. The mind is not explained only by listing the entities it knows. It is explained by analyzing how objects are made present: as seen, imagined, remembered, universally conceived, singularly designated, intuitively present, abstractively considered, divinely known, or angelically grasped.

5. Sensory cognition: active reception, attention, and appearance

Auriol rejects the idea that sensation is merely passive reception. The reception of a species or impression from an external object is necessary for ordinary perception, but it is not sufficient for cognition. The cognitive power must actively make the object appear. Lička illustrates this with Auriol’s example of open eyes that receive visual input while the person is mentally absorbed elsewhere: the object may causally affect the sense organ, but unless the cognitive power attends or responds, the object does not properly appear.

This makes Auriol’s perception theory both causal and active. The object contributes species or impressions; the sense power contributes cognitive animation, attention, or manifestation. Lička’s study of Auriol on perception describes this as an active theory of perception in which the senses participate in producing perceptual content, while that content remains directed to something external rather than being merely internal.

This active theory also explains illusions, dreams, and sensory error. Auriol holds that sensory cognition can sometimes be intuitive even when the object does not exist externally. The SEP notes his treatment of sensory illusions and his distinction between cognition’s apparent object and the object’s actual external existence. Adriaenssen’s work on skepticism emphasizes that Auriol’s apparent-being theory allows natural intuitive cognitions of nonexistents, but argues that this does not automatically make Auriol more skeptical than his opponents.

Dreaming is especially revealing. Martin’s study explains that Auriol treats dreams as a kind of reverse movement of imaginary intentions from imagination and common sense toward particular senses. Sleepers can, in a qualified but serious sense, see, hear, or touch absent objects. This does not mean the absent object has real presence; it means the cognitive system can generate apparent presence through internal sensory-imaginative processes.

So Auriol’s perceptual philosophy is not a simple direct realism. It is better described as active direct intentionalism. The object perceived is the thing itself as appearing, but appearing requires cognitive activity and can occur in defective or objectless cases.

6. Concepts, universals, and the human intellect

Auriol’s conceptualism begins from the thesis that only individuals exist extramentally. There is no real universal humanity existing apart from individual humans. Universals arise through intellectual cognition, but they are grounded in similarities or rationes found in singular things. The mind does not invent universality from nothing; it fabricates universal concepts from the intelligible content of singulars.

The universal concept is not an inner word or image that first becomes the object of thought. Auriol rejects the idea that concepts are internal representations serving as immediate objects. Instead, the thing or things themselves appear universally. The universal concept “human being,” for instance, is not a mental picture stored inside the intellect; it is the relevant singular humans, or the common ratio of human nature in them, as appearing under a universal mode.

Human intellectual cognition, however, is not pure intellect operating in isolation. In this life, it depends on phantasms. Auriol takes seriously the Aristotelian thesis that the embodied human intellect understands by turning toward phantasms. Lička’s Czech study notes that for Auriol, when the human being understands a rose, the universal does not appear in a purely separated intellectual field but is blended with or supported by the particular imaginative presentation.

This dependence on phantasy is a defining mark of the human mind. It explains why human thought is limited, why we often attend to one object at a time, why universals are reached through sensory-imaginative mediation, and why our intellectual grasp of singulars is indirect. God and angels are not constrained in this way, which is why they are philosophically indispensable to Auriol’s theory rather than theological decorations.

7. Singular cognition: humans, angels, and God

Auriol’s theory of singular cognition is one of the best places to see the unity of his philosophy of mind. Since only singulars exist extramentally, cognition must in some way reach singulars. Yet human intellectual cognition in this life does not directly intuit singulars intellectually. The human intellect knows singulars through cooperation with imagination: the phantasm designates or “signs” the singular in a particular position, and the intellect reasons from that imaginative designation.

Friedman’s reconstruction emphasizes that Auriol’s account is motivated partly by theological and angelological cases. God and guardian angels must know singulars, and their ability to do so clarifies what human intellect lacks. Human beings form concepts of singulars only through the cooperation of imagination and intellect; phantasm is necessary. The singular is “signed” or designated in a unique imaginative position, and the intellect discursively relates to that designation.

This explains a central asymmetry. The embodied human intellect can directly grasp universals, but its grasp of singulars is mediated by imagination. For God and angels, direct intellectual cognition of singulars is possible. The SEP similarly notes Auriol’s pessimism about embodied human reason: humans in this life lack direct intellectual intuition of the world and of God, whereas angels, the blessed, and God can directly intellectually cognize singulars.

Auriol’s singular cognition theory is thus not merely an epistemological detail. It is a theory of embodied mind. Human thought is structurally dependent on phantasm, spatial designation, and temporal-discursive mediation. Angelic and divine minds show what intellectual cognition can be when those constraints are removed.

8. Species, cognitive acts, and the mental word

Auriol’s attack on representational intermediaries includes his treatment of intelligible species, cognitive acts, and the mental word. He does not simply deny that there are species or likenesses in finite cognition. Rather, he denies that the species is a distinct inner object that terminates cognition before the external thing is reached. In Martin’s account, Auriol argues that the species and the act of cognition are not really distinct; the same reality may be called a species in one respect and an act of intellection in another.

This is crucial. Auriol is not a crude eliminativist about all cognitive mediation. Created cognition involves likeness, impression, or similitude. But these are not mental screens. They are not what the mind first knows. The act goes directly to the rose, not to an internal rose-image. The species is the cognitive act’s way of being informed or assimilated, not an object interposed between intellect and world.

French scholarship on Capreolus’s critique of Auriol highlights how radical this looked to Thomist opponents. Auriol’s concept is not an intramental accidental form serving as an immanent terminus; it is the thing itself in esse apparens. Capreolus objects that this threatens to dissolve the Thomistic mental word and creates a metaphysically obscure “apparent being” that may fail to secure realism.

The disagreement is not merely terminological. It concerns whether cognition must involve an immanent mental product — a word, species, or concept inside the soul — that mediates access to reality. Auriol’s answer is largely no: such intermediaries threaten to make cognition about mental objects rather than real things. But his own alternative faces the question of how apparent being can be neither a real inner object nor an empty façon de parler.

9. Intellectual memory and non-occurrent cognition

Auriol also has a sophisticated account of intellectual memory. Martin argues that Auriol posits intellectual memory as a place or power retaining non-occurrent intelligible similitudes or species, analogous in some respects to sensitive memory. However, Auriol resists excessive multiplication of faculties: he does not sharply distinguish intellect’s retentive and apprehensive powers as separate real faculties, nor does he sharply distinguish species from act as separate realities.

The problem is this: if the intellect retains intelligible likenesses, why are we not always consciously thinking everything we remember? Auriol’s answer appeals to apparent being and attention. A retained likeness does not by itself produce conscious cognition. The object must be brought under the gaze of the mind, often with the help of imagination, attention, and will. Martin stresses that non-occurrent similitudes can remain dormant until activated by phantasia or attentional direction.

This anticipates a distinction familiar in contemporary terms, though not in contemporary vocabulary: possession of cognitive content is not the same as occurrent awareness. Auriol can say that the mind retains intellectual likenesses without saying that all retained content is consciously appearing. Consciousness requires the object’s actual apparent presence.

10. Attention, will, and cognitive control

Auriol gives the will and attention a significant role in cognition without collapsing perception into voluntarism. Fornasieri’s work on attention and impressions presents Auriol as holding that sensation and intellection are both passive and active: passive because they receive species or impressions from extramental objects, active because the cognitive powers process these impressions and produce the intentional object. The will can focus intellectual attention and influence which concepts are formed.

Martin’s analysis is especially helpful here. Auriol thinks the “gaze of the mind” is subject to will, desires, and imaginative triggers, but he does not think the will can simply command sensory reality at will. External sensory impressions are natural; we cannot simply decide to see red as blue. Cognitive attention is governed by appetite and will, but it remains constrained by sensory and imaginative material.

This gives Auriol a nuanced philosophy of cognitive agency. We are not passive mirrors. We select, attend, combine, and conceptualize. But we are also not sovereign creators of appearances. Our cognitive life is a negotiated product of object, organ, species, phantasm, attention, appetite, and intellect.

11. Emotion, appetite, and animal mind

Auriol’s philosophy of mind is not limited to intellect. His treatment of animals, inner senses, and appetite shows that he takes nonhuman cognition seriously. Lička’s Czech study explains that animals have external senses, imagination, and an estimative power that allows them to apprehend practical significances, such as threat or usefulness. The classic sheep-and-wolf example appears here: the sheep does not merely see a colored shape; it apprehends the wolf as dangerous, and this apprehension moves appetite and action.

This means animals are not mere sensory machines. They have a structured cognitive-affective life. They do not possess universal intellectual cognition, judgment, or syllogistic reasoning in the human sense, but their inner senses organize perception in action-guiding ways. Auriol’s mind theory therefore includes a graded account of cognition: sensory appearance, imaginative appearance, estimative appearance, intellectual appearance, angelic intellectual appearance, and divine cognition.

Auriol’s account of freedom also extends further than one might expect. In his debate with Henry of Ghent, Auriol holds that the act of experiencing pleasure or satisfaction — actus complacentiae or delectatio — is formally free, and that children and animals can participate in a kind of voluntary or spontaneous activity even if they lack full rational election and dominion over acts.

This is important for philosophy of mind because it resists an overly intellectualist model of agency. Freedom is not located only in explicit rational choice. It begins in affective orientation, delight, attraction, and spontaneous self-movement. Human rational election is a higher form of agency, but not the only form of minded agency.

12. Intuitive and abstractive cognition

Auriol’s distinction between intuitive and abstractive cognition is central to his account of presence, absence, and object-existence. He does not define intuitive cognition simply as cognition of an existing object and abstractive cognition as cognition of a nonexistent object. Rather, the distinction is phenomenological: intuitive cognition presents the object as present or existence-involving, while abstractive cognition considers the object without that same presentational force.

This allows Auriol to say something striking: there can be intuitive sensory cognition of nonexistents, as in certain hallucinations, dreams, or divinely caused appearances. The object appears as present even though it lacks corresponding external existence. That is why Auriol’s theory attracted skeptical objections from figures such as Chatton and Wodeham. Adriaenssen’s study argues, however, that Auriol’s apparent-being theory does not by itself make him uniquely vulnerable to skepticism; the problem of error also affects alternative theories.

For embodied humans, intellectual intuitive cognition is sharply limited. Auriol denies that human beings in this life directly intellectually intuit singulars or God. Our intellectual cognition is typically abstractive and phantasm-mediated. Angels, the blessed, and God occupy the other side of the taxonomy: they can have forms of intellectual presence unavailable to embodied human cognition.

13. Angelic minds and the minds of the blessed

Auriol’s angelology is philosophically indispensable because angels show what created intellect can be without bodily phantasy. The human mind is not the default model of all mind. It is a hybrid cognitive system: sensory, imaginative, intellectual, appetitive, embodied, and temporally constrained. Angelic intellect clarifies what remains when the sensory-imaginative apparatus is absent.

Martin notes that one of Auriol’s important texts on these questions is Sentences II, distinction 11, which asks whether species and intellection are distinct in angelic intellect. This is not an isolated theological puzzle. It tests the general theory of species, act, and apparent being in a case where the intellect is finite but not humanly embodied.

The angelic case also matters for singular cognition. Friedman emphasizes that God and guardian angels must know singulars, while human beings know singulars through phantasm-dependent designation. Thus angelic cognition is not merely “human cognition minus body”; it has a different relation to singulars, one not dependent on the human imagination’s spatial signing.

The blessed likewise matter because they represent perfected created cognition. In this life, humans lack direct intellectual intuition of God and singulars; the blessed can have cognitive access beyond the limits of embodied phantasm. Martin points to Auriol’s Quodlibet q.8 on whether a created similitude is required in beatific vision, again showing that the question of blessed cognition is also a question about the structure of finite cognition as such.

Auriol’s hierarchy of minds can therefore be stated as a hierarchy of modes of appearing. Human beings are not less rational because they have bodies; rather, their rationality is constituted through bodily phantasm. Angels and blessed souls are not irrelevant exceptions; they disclose the range of possible created intellectual appearance beyond embodied mediation.

14. The divine mind: simplicity, connotation, and ideas

God’s mind is the supreme boundary case. For Auriol, divine cognition is not a mental act added to God, nor a relation that multiplies divine being. God’s cognition is the deity itself. Taieb’s reconstruction explicitly distinguishes created cognition, which involves cognitive power and likeness, from divine cognition, which is identical with the divine essence.

This bears directly on Auriol’s theory of divine ideas. Auriol is concerned that positing many divine ideas as intermediaries threatens divine simplicity. Paladini’s study of exemplar causality argues that Auriol challenges divine ideas understood as mediating exemplars: the divine essence is the only object of God’s knowledge and the only exemplar for creation. God knows creatures through multiple connotations of the one divine essence, not through a plurality of really distinct ideal models.

This is not an anti-intellectual account of God. It is a rigorous attempt to preserve both divine simplicity and divine knowledge of plurality. The divine essence can be the one equivocal exemplar of many created things. The plurality lies not in God as a set of really distinct ideas, but in connoted relations to creatures. In Auriol’s mind theory, God is therefore cognition without species, without phantasm, without created similitude, without mental multiplicity, and without temporal discursiveness.

The problem of future contingents sharpens this. The SEP explains that Auriol holds God does not know the future as future in the human temporal sense, but “indistantly,” abstracting from temporal distance. Fedriga’s Italian study of divine mind and contingency similarly presents Auriol’s treatment of divine knowledge of individuals and future contingents as involving modes of cognition, denomination, and an analogy with human cognitive abilities concerning temporal events — but without reducing divine cognition to human temporal imagination.

Here again, theological doctrine is philosophically productive. Human minds know temporally, discursively, and phantasm-dependently. God knows simply, eternally, and through the divine essence. The contrast clarifies what is contingent in human cognition and what belongs to cognition as such.

15. Comparative taxonomy of minds

Auriol’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a graded account of cognitive appearance:

Kind of being Cognitive structure Mode of appearing Principal limits or privileges Noncognitive beings No cognitive power Nothing appears to them They have real being but no intentional multiplication Animals External senses, common sense, imagination, memory, estimation, appetite Sensory, imaginative, and practical-evaluative appearance No universal intellectual cognition, but genuine action-guiding cognition Embodied humans Senses, inner senses, agent and possible intellect, will Universal and singular cognition through phantasm-mediated appearance No direct intellectual intuition of singulars or God in this life; often one-object attentional limitation Angels Created intellect without bodily phantasy Intellectual appearance not dependent on human phantasms Direct intellectual grasp of singulars possible, but still finite and created Blessed souls Perfected created cognition Intellectual vision beyond ordinary embodied limits Beatific cognition raises the question of created similitude and finite participation God Divine essence identical with cognition No created apparent-being mechanism; God knows through God’s own essence Absolute simplicity; no species, phantasms, or plurality of real divine ideas

This taxonomy shows why Auriol’s account should not be read as merely a human psychology. It is a comparative metaphysics of mind.

16. Systematic reconstruction: an Auriolian philosophy of mind

A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Auriol would include at least the following theses.

First, the appearance thesis: cognition is the making-present of an object under the mode of appearing. The object’s appearing is not an accidental psychological decoration; it is what cognition formally is.

Second, the direct-object thesis: the primary object of cognition is not an inner representation but the thing itself in apparent or intentional being. Auriol’s anti-representationalism is one of the most striking features of his mind theory.

Third, the connotative-identity thesis: the thing as cognized is not a second real thing. The difference between real being and apparent being is handled by connotation, denomination, and intentional mode, not by positing a new substantial or accidental object inside the mind.

Fourth, the singular-realist/conceptualist thesis: only singulars exist extramentally, but universals are genuine intentional achievements of the intellect grounded in similarities or rationes among singulars.

Fifth, the faculty-as-mode thesis: cognitive faculties are distinguished by modes of appearing rather than by wholly separate object domains. Seeing, imagining, remembering, and understanding differ because the same thing can appear in different ways.

Sixth, the active-receptive thesis: cognition is both receptive and active. External impressions and species matter, but cognitive powers must process, attend, and manifest. The mind is not a passive wax tablet.

Seventh, the embodiment thesis: human intellectual cognition in this life is phantasm-dependent. Human universality, singular designation, memory, and attention are all shaped by the imagination and inner senses.

Eighth, the attentional-volitional thesis: will, desire, and attention shape cognition, especially concept formation and the direction of the intellectual gaze, without giving the will arbitrary power over sensory appearances.

Ninth, the hierarchy-of-minds thesis: animals, humans, angels, blessed souls, and God are not accidental examples. They define the range of possible cognition: sensory, imaginative, estimative, intellectual, phantasm-bound, phantasm-free, finite, blessed, and divine.

Tenth, the fallibilist thesis: appearance does not guarantee real external existence. Auriol can explain dreams, illusions, and nonveridical intuitive cognition, but he must also answer skeptical worries about how apparent being connects to real being.

17. Problems and tensions

Auriol’s philosophy of mind is powerful because it tries to avoid the representational veil. But the price is metaphysical difficulty.

The first problem is the status of apparent being. If esse apparens is real, Auriol seems to multiply entities. If it is not real, one may ask how it explains cognition. Capreolus presses this kind of objection, arguing that Auriol’s apparent being is obscure, unnecessary, and threatens realism. Friedman and Moaté both identify similar tensions: Auriol wants continuity between the real object and the intentional object, but the very distinction between real and apparent being can look like a split.

The second problem is skepticism. If something can appear intuitively without existing, how do we know when appearance tracks reality? Auriol’s answer is not that error is impossible. Rather, he treats cognition as naturally world-directed while allowing exceptional cases of nonveridical appearance. Adriaenssen’s defense is that this does not make Auriol uniquely vulnerable, since rival theories also need to explain illusion, hallucination, and divine or supernatural cases.

The third problem is memory. If retained species and acts are not really distinct, Auriol must explain how non-occurrent retained content differs from conscious intellection. His answer appeals to the distinction between dormant likeness and actual apparent presence under the gaze of the mind, but this requires a sophisticated role for attention, phantasm, and will.

The fourth problem is divine plurality. If God knows infinitely many creatures without many ideas, how is plurality preserved? Auriol’s answer is connotation: creatures are known through the one divine essence under multiple connoted relations. This preserves simplicity but raises the question of whether connotation can bear all the explanatory weight traditionally assigned to divine ideas.

The fifth problem is the faculty distinction. Auriol’s appeal to modes of appearing is philosophically rich, but it may make faculty distinctions depend on phenomenology or first-person difference rather than on clean metaphysical divisions. This is not necessarily a defect; it may be one of Auriol’s most original moves. But it does make his psychology less tidy than more object-based Aristotelian taxonomies.

18. Non-Anglophone reception and why it matters

The non-English scholarship is not peripheral. French scholarship, especially Moaté’s work, highlights the Averroist background and the theory of two subjects of intentional being. This adds an important structural dimension often missed if Auriol is read only through later debates over direct realism and skepticism.

Italian scholarship, represented by Fedriga’s study of divine mind and contingency, foregrounds the relation between divine cognition, temporal events, and human phantasm-based cognition. This helps integrate Auriol’s philosophy of mind with his theology of providence rather than treating divine foreknowledge as a separate doctrinal puzzle.

Czech scholarship by Lička is valuable for reconstructing Auriol’s anthropology: life as cognition, active perception, faculty psychology, animal cognition, human dependence on phantasy, and the contrast with God and angels. It makes especially clear that Auriol’s question is not only “What is intellection?” but “What kind of living being is a human, given the way things appear to it?”

Spanish and Iberian scholastic reception, especially through discussions of Antonio Pérez, shows that Auriol’s theory of apparent being and connotation continued to matter after the medieval period. The later debate over whether apparent being is identical with the extramental thing insofar as cognized, and how intelligible species relate to acts of cognition, confirms that Auriol’s account remained a live option in early modern scholastic philosophy of mind.

German-language work on Auriol, including Paladini on divine ideas and Wöller on theology and science, helps situate Auriol’s noetics within broader questions of scientific knowledge, theology, and exemplar causality. Auriol’s theory of mind is inseparable from his account of theology as a form of cognition and from his attempt to preserve divine simplicity while explaining created multiplicity.

19. Final synthesis

Auriol’s philosophy of mind can be summarized as an appearance-based, anti-representational, conceptualist, embodied, and hierarchical theory of cognition.

It is appearance-based because cognition is fundamentally apparere: the object’s being present to a cognitive power.

It is anti-representational because Auriol resists making inner species, concepts, or mental words the immediate objects of knowledge.

It is conceptualist because universals have no extramental real existence as universals, though they are grounded in the intelligible features of singular things.

It is embodied because human intellection in this life depends on phantasm, imagination, sensory history, attention, and bodily limitation.

It is hierarchical because cognition differs across animals, humans, angels, blessed souls, and God. The theological cases are not distractions. They are necessary for seeing what belongs to cognition as such and what belongs only to embodied human cognition.

The result is a powerful alternative to both naïve realism and inner-representation theories. Auriol’s mind is not primarily a box of ideas. It is a living power by which beings become present as appearing. Animals live within sensory and estimative appearances; humans universalize through phantasm-bound intellect; angels and the blessed reveal finite intellect beyond embodiment; God is perfect cognition identical with the divine essence, knowing all things without species, phantasms, or multiplied ideas.

Taken this way, Auriol is less a precursor of one modern doctrine than a systematic alternative: mind is not fundamentally a container of representations, but a graded power of making beings present.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
